ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Beef, grilled) used as closest parent; no specific yakiniku plate composite code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for Beef Yakiniku Plate as a composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 g, comprising roughly 150 g of grilled marinated beef slices and 200 g of steamed white rice, as typically served at a Japanese gyudon/yakiniku chain restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, short loin, all grades, cooked, broiled (USDA SR Legacy #13463) combined with Cooked white rice, short-grain (USDA SR Legacy #20057); yakiniku marinade contribution estimated from standard recipe proportions
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Beef Yakiniku Plate is a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for grilled beef (approx. 43% by weight, ~150 g per 350 g serving) and cooked short-grain white rice (approx. 57% by weight, ~200 g per 350 g serving), with minor marinade contribution (soy sauce, mirin, sugar, sesame oil) estimated at standard recipe proportions. Per-100g values are derived from the blended composite. This is a medium-confidence estimate; direct HPB or USDA Branded Food entry for Sukiya Beef Yakiniku Plate was not available.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Foster-Powell K, Holt SHA, Brand-Miller JC; white rice (cooked, short-grain) GI ~64 used as dominant carbohydrate component anchor
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: Beef Yakiniku Plate has no direct GI measurement in the University of Sydney GI Database. The GI of 64 is assigned based on the dominant carbohydrate source being cooked short-grain white rice (GI ~64, medium category), which constitutes approximately 57% of the plate by weight and provides virtually all glycemic carbohydrates. The beef and marinade components contribute negligible additional glycemic load. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI 64 × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (64 × 71.75 g carbohydrate in 350 g serving) / 100 ≈ 45.9, rounded to 46.5 accounting for marinade sugars. This is an analogous estimate; direct GI testing of this composite dish has not been performed.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce marinade
hypertension.guidance: Each 350 g serving provides approximately 1085 mg sodium, representing nearly half the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium soy sauce or limit portion size to manage sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index driven by white rice component
type2_diabetes.guidance: The plate carries a GI of approximately 64 (medium) and a high glycemic load (~46) per serving due to the substantial white rice portion; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider reducing the rice portion, substituting with brown rice if available, and pairing with non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat and sodium from beef and soy sauce marinade
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The beef component contributes saturated fat (~2.0 g per 100 g) and the soy sauce marinade adds significant sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion frequency, choose leaner beef cuts when possible, and monitor overall saturated fat and sodium intake within daily targets.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Yakiniku Plate (牛焼肉プレート, Gyū Yakiniku Purēto) is a Japanese grilled beef dish served as a plate meal, typically consisting of thinly sliced beef marinated in a sweet-savory sauce of soy sauce, mirin, sake, sugar, garlic, and sesame oil, grilled over high heat until caramelised, and served alongside a generous portion of steamed short-grain white rice. The dish is a staple of Japanese yakiniku and gyudon chain restaurants such as Sukiya, where it is offered as an affordable, satisfying meal. The beef is tender and slightly charred at the edges, with a rich umami flavour from the soy-mirin marinade and a subtle sweetness from caramelised sugars. The rice provides a neutral, starchy base that absorbs the savoury meat juices. Nutritionally, the plate is a moderate-energy meal (~612 kcal per 350 g serving) providing meaningful protein (~37.8 g per serving) from the beef, with carbohydrates predominantly from white rice. Sodium content is elevated due to the soy sauce marinade, making portion awareness important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The medium glycemic index (~64) and high glycemic load per serving require attention for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is dairy-free but contains soy and gluten allergens from the marinade. It is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans.

What is yakiniku sauce made of?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Beef Yakiniku Plate as a composite dish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA Branded Foods for Sukiya Singapore. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for grilled beef and cooked short-grain white rice at representative proportions, with marinade contribution estimated from standard recipe data. GI is analogously estimated from the dominant carbohydrate source (white rice, GI 64) per the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence reflects the composite estimation approach rather than a direct source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA SR Legacy grilled beef and cooked short-grain white rice data; no direct HPB or branded-food entry for Sukiya Beef Yakiniku Plate was available at time of processing. 2. Serving size of 350 g is an estimate based on typical Japanese chain restaurant plate portions (150 g beef + 200 g rice); actual Sukiya portion may vary. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reliably reported in the USDA SR Legacy composite sources used for this estimation. 4. GI of 64 is an analogous estimate anchored to cooked short-grain white rice per the University of Sydney GI Database; direct GI testing of this composite dish has not been performed. 5. Halal status is listed as false as Sukiya Singapore's halal certification status was not confirmed at time of processing; consumers should verify directly with the restaurant. 6. The cuisine_tags in the input JSON included 'Japanese Curry' and 'Grilled Chicken Skewers' which are not relevant to this dish and have been disregarded as field survey platform tagging artefacts. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and carry medium confidence.
